coredata.dc:title: A low-cost platinum-free electrocatalyst based on carbon quantum dots decorated Ni–Cu hierarchical nanocomposites for hydrogen evolution reaction
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2020 Hydrogen Energy Publications LLC. Published by Elsevier Ltd. All rights reserved.
coredata.prism:publisher: Hydrogen Energy Publications LLC. Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.dc:creator: Javan, Hakimeh
Asghari, Elnaz
Ashassi-Sorkhabi, Habib
Haghighi, Masoumeh Moradi-
coredata.dc:description: The effective Ni–Cu bimetallic nanocomposite was deposited on a glassy carbon electrode, GCE, that modified with carbon quantum dots, CQDs. The deposition process was done by one-step and controllable electrochemical method in an electrolyte of nickel and copper sulfate. The structural properties of composite studied by techniques such as X-ray diffraction, XRD, energy dispersive X-ray analysis, EDX, field emission scanning electron microscopy, FESEM, and transmission electron microscopy, TEM. Ni–Cu/RCQDs nanocomposite was applied as a cathode for catalysis of hydrogen evolution reaction, HER, in acidic media by cyclic voltammetry, CV, linear sweep voltammetry, LSV, chronoamperometry, CA, and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy, EIS. The onset potential, Eonset, for the evolution of hydrogen at the current density of −10 mA cm−2 for Ni–Cu/RCQDs was −230 mV vs. SHE that had a 100 mV shift to positive voltages in comparison with Ni–Cu catalyst. It can be related to the synergistic effect between metallic nanoparticles. V. dec−1, respectively.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Bimetallic nanocomposite
Carbon quantum dots
Controllable method
Onset potential
Synergistic effect

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of electrocatalysts on glassy carbon electrodes
Electrochemical behavior of electrodes by cyclic voltammetry
Electrocatalytic performance of the electrodes by linear sweep voltammetry and polarization measurement
Evaluation of the stability of the electrodes by chronoamperometry tests
Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy studies of electrodes during HER

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: XRD pattern of Ni–Cu and Ni–Cu/RCQDs samples.
FESEM images of Ni/GCE in two different magnifications.
FESEM image of Ni–Cu/GCE in three different magnifications.
A) FESEM image b) EDX pattern of prepared Ni–Cu/CQDs/GCE and c) TEM image of Ni–Cu/RCQDs catalysts.
Cyclic voltammograms of prepared electrodes in 5 mM Fe(CN)6 and 0.5 M KCl solution at scan rate of 50 mV/s.
Linear sweep voltammograms of the electrodes Ni/GCE, Ni–Cu/GCE and Ni–Cu/RCQDs/GCE at a scan rate of 5 mV/s in the 0.5 M solution of H2SO4.
Cathodic Tafel plots for HER on Ni–Cu/GCE and Ni–Cu/RCQDs/GCE.
LSV curves for Ni–Cu/GCE (a) and Ni–Cu/RCQDs/GCE (b) at several temperatures and a scan rate of 5 mV/s, (c) Arrhenius plots based on I at E = −0.15 V versus reciprocal of temperature.
Chronoamperometry plots of electrodes in 0.5 M H2SO4 at a constant voltage of −0.35 V vs. Ag/AgCl.
(a & b) Nyquist diagram and (aʹ & bʹ) Bode plots of Ni–Cu/GCE (red curves) and Ni–Cu/RCQDs/GCE (black curves) in three different voltages versus Ag/AgCl and (c) their fitted equivalent circuit.
Kinetic parameters of HER in 0.5 M H2SO4 solution for this study and some other studies.

row.entry: E (mV, vs. RHE) at i = 10 mA. cm−2

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The EIS parameters obtained from the experimental data for Ni–Cu/GCE and Ni–Cu/RCQD/GCE electrodes at three cathodic potentials.

head.ce:title: A low-cost platinum-free electrocatalyst based on carbon quantum dots decorated Ni–Cu hierarchical nanocomposites for hydrogen evolution reaction

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Electrochemistry Research Laboratory, Physical Chemistry Department, Faculty of Chemistry, University of Tabriz, Tabriz, Iran
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Electrochemistry Research Laboratory, Physical Chemistry Department, Faculty of Chemistry, University of Tabriz, Tabriz, Iran

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Shenyang National Laboratory for Materials Science

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Shenyang National Laboratory for Materials Science, Northeastern University, Shenyang 110819, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The effective Ni–Cu bimetallic nanocomposite was deposited on a glassy carbon electrode, GCE, that modified with carbon quantum dots, CQDs. The deposition process was done by one-step and controllable electrochemical method in an electrolyte of nickel and copper sulfate. The structural properties of composite studied by techniques such as X-ray diffraction, XRD, energy dispersive X-ray analysis, EDX, field emission scanning electron microscopy, FESEM, and transmission electron microscopy, TEM. Ni–Cu/RCQDs nanocomposite was applied as a cathode for catalysis of hydrogen evolution reaction, HER, in acidic media by cyclic voltammetry, CV, linear sweep voltammetry, LSV, chronoamperometry, CA, and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy, EIS. The onset potential, Eonset, for the evolution of hydrogen at the current density of −10 mA cm−2 for Ni–Cu/RCQDs was −230 mV vs. SHE that had a 100 mV shift to positive voltages in comparison with Ni–Cu catalyst. It can be related to the synergistic effect between metallic nanoparticles. V. dec−1, respectively.

ce:section.ce:para: Fossil fuels duo to some disadvantages such as environmental pollution, non-renewable, unsustainable and dangerous process, have to be replaced by other fuels. The new sources of energy should be easy to be stored and transported, be efficiently converted to other forms of energy, environmentally-friendly and safe and low cost [1,2] Hydrogen is one of the critical, fundamental, and efficient fuels that has been attracted much attention during recent decades. It has the highest energy content in comparison with common fuels and has the most abundance in the world [3–7]. Recently, the hydrogen evolution reaction, HER, has been studied wieldy in electrochemistry [8–11]. The primary and fundamental challenge that all of the energy systems are involved with it, is increasing the efficiency of energy production. The electrodes used to improve the efficiency of this reaction must contain a combination of some features such as acceptable stability, high catalytic activity (i.e. low overpotential), considerable active surface area, and low cost [12].

In order to minimize the need for catalytic metals, that are usually expensive, is to load them on a supporting material instead of using the bulk metal. It increases the active surface area of the electrode. Also leads to decrease the amount of required metal [5]. Carbon-based supports like carbon nanofibers [13,14], carbon nanotubes [15–17], graphene oxide [18], and carbon quantum dots, CQDs, are examples. CQDs, because of their high porosity, have been considered as a new kind of carbonic supporting materials in the field of electrochemistry [19–22]. Also, the diversity of synthesis processes, cost-effective precursors and optical, and electrical properties were extremely distinguished this type of support material from the other kinds of carbon nanomaterials [23]. The application of binary metals as a catalyst can be accounted as another important expedience for facilitating of HER. The most studied compounds for electrodes are alloys of nickel. Ni–Cu composites are one of the effective candidates for using as a catalyst for the evolution of hydrogen [24,25]. The electrodes made of this bimetallic composite are noble metals-free and are considered as an in-expensive replacement of noble metal-based catalysts. By providing good chemical and electrical stability, considerable electrocatalytic activity and higher surface area these new catalysts can be considered as future catalyst candidates; while their individual metals may not show such characteristics. The presence of another metallic phase, in two metallic structures, caused to decreases the consuming the noble metal. In addition, it has an important effect on electrochemical properties with the synergistic effect between two metals. The second metal by the ligand electronic interaction and/or a strain in the synergism effect, can improve the electronic futures of the first one, [5,26,27].

The goal of this work is the development of non-platinum electrocatalysts for HER. Deposition of CQDs by one-step and facile electrochemical method on glassy carbon electrode can be count from one of the valuable novelty of this research work. Also application of RCQDs as a base catalyst with incorporated of bi-metallic Cu–Ni nanoparticles, as an effective electrocatalyst for evolution of hydrogen, is another novelty of this work that will be achieved.

NiSO4.6H2O, CuSO4.5H2O, H3BO3, H2SO4, HCl and KCl were purchased from Merck Co. All three-electrode electrochemical tests were done by an AUTOLAB PGSTAT30 potentiostat-galvanostat that equipped with a frequency response analyzer, FRA, module. Cyclic voltammetry, CV, linear sweep voltammetry, LSV, chronoamperometry, CA, and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy, EIS, techniques were utilized for electrochemical characteristics of the catalysts. The Nyquist plots were fitted to appropriate equivalent circuits using the ZView® (II) software and the corresponding extracted parameters are listed in Table 2. An array including a glassy carbon electrode, GCE, (d = 2 mm) a platinum plate and an Ag/AgCl (3 M KCl) as working, counter and reference electrodes respectively, was applied. Morphologies of electrodes were studied by field emission scanning electron microscopy, FESEM, (TESCAN, MIRA III, Czech Republic) and transmission electron microscopy, TEM, (model Zeiss-EM10C -Germany) at an accelerating voltage of 100 kV. Also, for more evaluation of prepared the X-ray diffraction, XRD, (Philips, PW1730, Netherlands) with a Cu lamp (<λ ≥ 0.1541 nm) operating at 40 kV and 30 mA, was used.

Synthesis of CQDs was done with a simple electrochemical route in a two-electrode system using the ADAK DC power supply [28]. Two graphite rods with diameter of 5.6 mm are immersed in ultrapure water and constant voltage in the range of 30–40 V is applied. Synthesis procedure was performed under stirring for several days till obtaining a dark brown mixture. The obtained mixture was then filtered and centrifuged (High-speed table top centrifuge) at 22,000 rpm [29,30].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of electrocatalysts on glassy carbon electrodes
ce:section.ce:para: In order to electrodeposition of CQDs (1.0 mg/mL) on GCE, they were dispersed in a 0.10 M KCl solution. Dispersion was completed by sonication for 20 min in the ultrasonic bath (BANDELIN SONOREX DIGITEC). The pH adjusted to 4.0 using a Metrohm 654 PH-meter. For deposition of CQDs on GCE, cyclic voltammetry was used in the potential range of −1.5 to 0.5 V (vs. Ag/AgCl) with a scan rate of 25 mV/s for 20 scans [28]. Also, for electrodeposition of nickel nanoparticles and nickel-copper nanocomposite, a constant current of −0.4 mA was applied for 300 s. The deposition bath consist of 0.40 M NiSO4. 6H2O, 0.04 M CuSO4. 5H2O, 0.10 M KCl, and 0.30 M H3BO3.

XRD technique was used to studying the crystallinity and composition of obtained electrocatalysts [31,32]. The XRD pattern of NiCu/RCQDs, has been shown in Fig. 1 ; it indicates a peak at ca. 2θ = 19° corresponding to the highly disordered carbon of RCQDs. Three peaks at 2θ = 36.23°, 41.15°, and 71.15°, are related to the (111), (200), (220) planes of Ni–Cu, which are nearly coincident with those seen in the Ni–Cu pattern in Refs. [33].

The FESEM images of electrodeposited Nickel nanoparticles, Ni-NPs, on the bare GCE has been shown in Fig. 2 . From Fig. 2a, an almost homogenously dispersed layer of Ni masses is observed on GCE. It can be deduced that these masses are consisted of several Ni particles with a variety of sizes from nano to micro.

In Fig. 3 the FESEM images of co-deposited Ni–Cu on bare GCE are seen. As it is seen, Ni particles are mostly disk-shaped; the copper masses are deposited as polygons, which are largely triangular [34,35]. In Fig. 4 a, FESEM image of Ni–Cu/RCQDs/GCE is presented. It is clear from this figure that underneath CQDs layer results in better distribution and smaller size of particles.

TEM image of the Ni–Cu/RCQDs catalyst material is also indicated in Fig. 4c; nickel and copper are distinguished as circular and sharp-edge particles respectively, dispersed on a matrix of carbon dots. Spherical nickel grains, and polygon copper particles that were not agglomerated, were detected with a relatively uniform size distribution [36–39].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electrochemical behavior of electrodes by cyclic voltammetry
ce:section.ce:para: The electrochemical behavior of bare GCE, Ni–Cu/GCE and Ni–Cu/RCQDs/GCE was investigated by cyclic voltammetry to explain their electron transfer process in the solution of 0.5 M KCl and 5 mM K4 [Fe(CN)6] at scan rate of 50 mVs−1 and potential ranges of −0.1 V–0.7 V, vs. Ag/AgCl. CV curves in Fig. 5 show a pair of well-defined quasi-reversible anodic and cathodic peaks. As it is seen from Fig. 5, peak potential, Ep, values aren't changed significantly. While, peak current densities, ip, increase remarkably for Ni–Cu/GCE compared with bare GCE. The ip values for Ni–Cu/RCQDs/GCE rises again. These changes are due to the variation of active surface area of the electrodes. So, the Rundles- Sevcik equation was used to estimate the approximate active surface area of the electrodes.

The electroactive area of the electrodes for a reversible process are calculated by Randles- Sevick equation.
(1)
i p
= 2.99
x
10 5
n A C
D ν
where i p, n, A, C, D, and ν are the anodic peak current density (A.cm−2), number of transferred electrons during the reaction, active surface area (cm2), concentration of the reagent (mol.cm−3), diffusion coefficient of the reactant and the scan rate (V.s−1), respectively. The redox reaction of K4 [Fe(CN)6] involves one-electron transfer (n = 1), and the diffusion coefficient which is assumed to be 6.30 × 10−6 cm2 s−1 according to literatures [40,41]. So the calculated electroactive surface area for Ni–Cu/RCQD/GCE is 15.87 cm2 that is higher than Ni–Cu/GCE (9.45 cm2). It is obvious that the active surface area of Ni–Cu/RCQD/GCE is almost 1.5 times higher than Ni–Cu/GCE which is because of the incorporation of RCQDs layer prior to deposition of upper Ni–Cu layer and capacitance current of CQDs [42,43].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electrocatalytic performance of the electrodes by linear sweep voltammetry and polarization measurement
ce:section.ce:para: The electrocatalytic activity for the HER of the prepared electrodes was investigated in the 0.5 M H2SO4 solution by three-electrochemical system with LSV technique. The plots of different electrodes have been illustrated in Fig. 6 ; as it can be observed, the onset potentials of HER for Ni/GCE and Ni–Cu/GCE are much more negative than Ni–Cu/RCQDs/GCE. In other words, in a given current density (we assumed −10 mA cm−2) the corresponding overpotential is more positive for Ni–Cu/RCQDs/GCE (at the assumed current density it is −230, −330 and −420 mV for Ni–Cu/RCQDs/GCE, Ni–Cu/GCE and Ni/GCE respectively). It means that the presence of RCQDs in the structure of the electrode results in higher current densities and it reflects the improved electrocatalytic performance of that electrode. More dispersed Ni–Cu particles on RCQDs decorated GCE, higher active surface area, due to the presence of CQDs, as well as synergistic catalytic performance of Ni and Cu particles with RCQDs may be the main reasons of such a significant difference.

Tafel analysis was performed for having a deeper insight and more detailed description of the electrochemical behavior of the electrodes. Fig. 7 indicates the cathodic Tafel plots of the electrodes in a 0.5 M H2SO4 solution. Tafel slopes, as well as the potentials corresponding to I = 10 mA cm−2 [44], current densities at over voltages of −200 mV, exchange current densities, I °, and activation energy, E a, for HER are listed in Table 1
. The evolution of H2 molecules on the surface of the electrodes follows the well-known multi-step electrochemical process. Generally, the mechanisms of HER in acid media could be explained as follows [45–48]:
a)
Adsorption of Proton with charge transfer (Volmer reaction):
(2)
H ₃
O +
+ e →
H
a d s
+ H ₂ O
b)
Combination of adsorbed hydrogen atoms (Tafel reaction):
(3)
H
a d s
+
H
a d s
→
H 2
c)
Combination of an adsorbed hydrogen atom and a hydronium ion (Heyrovsky reaction):
(4)
2 )
H
a d s
+ H ₃
O
+
+ e →
H 2
+ H ₂ O

HER begins with reduction of protons and their adsorption on surface (the Volmer reaction). The process is then continued b the combination of two adsorbed hydrogen atoms that adsorbed on surface of electrode (the Tafel reaction) or with the combination of a mentioned adsorbed hydrogen atoms with a hydronium ion (the Heyrovsky reaction) leading to the generation of molecular hydrogen. The steps can vary with the kinetics of the reaction. For HER at small overpotentials, the Tafel slope would be about 30 mV.dec−1 if the reaction proceeded through the Volmer reaction followed by the Tafel reaction so called “Volmer- Tafel” mechanism. At high overvoltages, the reaction is limited by the Tafel reaction. For the Tafel mechanism, at high overpotentials a Tafel slope of about 117.1 mV.dec−1 is expected.

The tafel slopes of 117.1 and 83.5 mV.dec−1 for the Ni–Cu/GCE, and Ni–Cu/RCQDs/GCE s, were obtained respectively. The calculated amounts for the Tafel slopes are considerably higher than the expected amounts at lower overpotentials but they are close to the amount of slope at high overvoltage [4,49,50]. The exchange current density, I ₀, derived from Tafel equation, for Ni–Cu/RCQDs/GCE is about 0.06 mA cm−2; that is higher than the I ₀ of Ni–Cu/GCE (0.005 mA cm−2). In fact, the enhanced active surface area for adsorption of proton ions provided by underneath RCQDs leads to the higher current densities due to the increased overall reaction [51,52].

HER mechanism was more studied by calculating the apparent activation energies, E a. For most reactions, the Arrhenius equation is used to determine the E a values [48]:
(5)
k
( T )
= A
e
−
E a
R T
Where k (T) is the rate constant, A is the pre-exponential factor, T is temperature and R is the universal gases constant. In the electrochemical reactions, the current density, I, is considered as a measure of the reaction kinetics; hence, it is common to assume I values proportional with k (T) and extend the Arrhenius equation for these values instead of actual k (T). Therefore, with a modified Arrhenius equation for electrochemical processes, we can estimate an apparent activation energy, E app, derived from the current densities obtained at different temperatures (Fig. 8 ) [53].
(6)
E a p p = R
d
(
l n I
)
d
(
1 / T
)

From the slope of the plots in Fig. 7c (slope = −E app/2.303R) the E app values were calculated. The average E app values are also listed in Table 1 and it is observed that they are significantly less for both Ni–Cu/GCE and Ni–Cu/RCQDs/GCE than those previously reported. The E app is also lowered with the decoration of the substrate with a very thin layer of RCQDs. It means that they not only enhance the active surface area, and affect the adsorption of precursors on the surface, but also they show some extent of intrinsic catalytic behavior; the later property has been reflected in the lowered E app values for Ni–Cu/RCQDs/GCE compared with Ni–Cu/GCE.
From Fig. 8a and b it is also obvious that at higher temperatures, the onset potentials shift toward positive value [47,48,54].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Evaluation of the stability of the electrodes by chronoamperometry tests
ce:section.ce:para: One of the critical factors about catalytic behavior is the stability of the electrodes. This feature has been investigated by chronoamperometry tests in 0.50 M H2SO4 solution at a constant cathodic potential of −0.35 V, vs. Ag/AgCl for the 2000 s (Fig. 9 ). The higher steady-state current of density of Ni–Cu/RCQDs/GCE compared with Ni–Cu/GCE, and Ni-NPs/GCE is confirmed the improve catalytic behavior of the Ni–Cu electrode in presence of RCQDs and synergistic effect between nickel and copper nanoparticles with RCQDs. The delayed steady-state current for Ni–Cu/RCQDs/GCE can be attributed to higher porosity provided by the RCQDs sub-layer.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy studies of electrodes during HER
ce:section.ce:para:
In order to study the electron transfer kinetic properties of the fabricated electrodes, EIS analysis was carried out in acidic media with the perturbation voltage of 10.0 mV (rms) sinusoidal voltage in the frequency between 100 kHz and 100 mHz at three applied voltages. The potentials were selected according to LSV results. Fig. 10 a and b illustrate the Nyquist diagrams and Bode plots of Ni–Cu/GCE and Ni–Cu/RCQDs/GCE at three cathodic voltages. The experimental data were fitted to the electrochemical equivalent circuit shown in Fig. 10c that is a Randles model with a constant phase element, CPE and the results were The parameters R s and R ct represent series and charge transfer resistances, respectively. Generally, R s is attributed to the electrical transport properties of the catalyst, and R ct is associated with the kinetics of charge transfer at the catalyst/electrolyte interface [61,62]. CPE is used instead of an ideal double layer capacitance, C dl, in systems in which there is an activation energy distribution on the surface of the electrode. In such systems, the ideal capacitance cannot describe the overall process. The result is a non-ideal depressed capacitive loop in the Nyquist plots. CPE is defined and related to C dl using two parameters including the admittance, Y ₀, and power, n, of as following equation [63,64]:
(7)
C d l =
(
(
Y ∘
R c t
)
1 / n
)
/
R c t

As it was expected, the R ct has shown remarkable decrease with increase of the applied voltage. At all applied potentials, the Ni–Cu/RCQDs/GCE exhibits lower R ct values compared with Ni–Cu/GCE; it indicates an improved electrocatalytic activity due to the presence of RCQDs [50,65].

Bimetallic Ni–Cu nanocomposites on glassy carbon electrode modified by RCQDs were prepared through facile, one-step, and controllable electrochemical method in an aqueous solution of nickel and copper salts. The morphologies of prepared structures was studied by some characterization tests like XRD, EDS, and FESEM. In addition, the performance of optimized electrodes in HER system was investigated in acidic media at room temperature by different electrochemical techniques. A current density of 10 mA cm−2 for Ni–Cu/RCQDs/GCE was obtained with a low overpotential of −230 mV, corresponding to a small Tafel slope of 83.5 mV.dec−1. The generated current from Ni–Cu/RCQDs/GCE nanocomposite was higher than a Ni–Cu/GCE and pure Ni electrode, which was attributed to (i) the high active surface area provided by RCQDs and (ii) the synergistic effect between Ni and Cu. The improved catalytic treatment the proposed nanocomposite for HER in acidic media was confirmed by increased cathodic current densities, decreased overvoltages, lower activation energies, good stabilities, and reduced charge transfer resistances.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This project was carried out in “Electrochemistry Research Laboratory” of “University of Tabriz’‘. The authors would like to thank “University of Tabriz” and “Iran National Science Foundation (INSF)” for their financial supports of this project (Grant Number 96001731).
